analogy: Think of "Change and Development in Rural Society" through the chapter's own opening images: Social Change and Development in India 42 Indian society is primarily a rural society though urbanisation is growing. The majority of India’s people live in rural areas (69 per cent, according to the 2011 Census). They make their living from agriculture or related occupations. This... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Change and Development in Rural Society is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that social Change and Development in India 42 Indian society is primarily a rural society though urbanisation is growing.

The text explains that the majority of India’s people live in rural areas (69 per cent, according to the 2011 Census).

The text explains that they make their living from agriculture or related occupations.

The text explains that this means that agricultural land is the most important productive resource for a great many Indians.

The text explains that land is also the most important form of property.

The text explains that but land is not just a ‘means of production’ nor just a ‘form of property’.

The text explains that nor is agriculture just a form of livelihood.

The text explains that many of our cultural practices and patterns can be traced to our agrarian backgrounds.

The text explains that you will recall from the earlier chapters how closely interrelated structural and cultural changes are.

The text explains that for example, most of the New Year festivals in different regions of India – such as Pongal in Tamil Nadu, Bihu in Assam, Baisakhi in Punjab and Ugadi in Karnataka to name just a few – actually celebrate the main harvest season and herald the beginning of a new agricultural season.

The text explains that different means of agriculture and related festivals.

The text explains that there is a close connection between agriculture and culture.

The text explains that the nature and practice of agriculture varies greatly across the different regions of the country.

The text explains that these variations are reflected in the different regional cultures.

The text explains that one can say that both the culture and social structure in rural India are closely bound up with agricultural and the agrarian way of life.

The text explains that agriculture is the single most important source of livelihood for the majority of the rural population.

The text explains that many activities that support agriculture and village life are also sources of livelihood for people in rural India.

The text explains that social Change and Development in India 44 4.1 AgrAriAn Structure: cASte And clASS in rurAl indiA Agricultural land is the single most important resource and form of property in rural society.

The text explains that because agricultural land is the most important productive resource in rural areas, access to land shapes the rural class structure.

The text explains that the rough correspondence between caste and class means that typically the upper and middle castes also had the best access to land and resources, and hence to power and privilege.

The text explains that in most regions of the country, a ‘proprietary caste’ group owns most of the resources and can command labour to work for them.

The text explains that but it is important to realise that this agrarian structure has changed enormously over time, from the pre-colonial to the There is a direct correspondence between agricultural productivity and the agrarian structure.

The text explains that discuss in terms of different access to resources, labour and occupation.

The text explains that while the same dominant castes were probably also cultivating castes in the pre-colonial period, they were not the direct owners of land.

The text explains that in other areas that were under direct British rule had what was called the raiyatwari system of land settlement (raiyat means cultivator in Telugu).

The text explains that because the colonial government dealt directly with the farmers or landlords, rather than through the overlords, the burden of taxation was less and cultivators had more incentive to invest in agriculture.

The text explains that as a result, only certain regions such as the Punjab, western U.P., coastal Andhra Pradesh, and parts of Tamil Nadu, received the first wave of the Green Revolution package.

The text explains that for landowners began to take back land from their tenants and cultivate it directly because cultivation was becoming more profitable.

The text explains that in many regions in the 19 th century, large tracts of land in Maharashtra were given over to cotton cultivation, and cotton farmers became directly linked to the world market.

The text explains that the ‘Deen Dayal Upadhyaya Gram Jyoti Yojna’ launched in 2014 is a laudable effort of the government of India in this direction.

The text explains that women are also emerging as the main source of agricultural labour, leading to the ‘feminisation of agricultural labour force.’ The insecurity of women is greater because they earn lower wages than men for similar work.

The text explains that social Change and Development in India 54 had direct effects on farmers and rural society.

The text explains that these agents are often the sole source of information for farmers about new seeds or cultivation practices, and of course they have an interest in selling their products.

The text explains that there are direct linkages between the situation of agricultural workers and their lack of upward socio-economic mobility.

The text explains that possible source: State of India’s Environment Reports, Reports from Centre for Science and Development and the magazine Down to Earth.

The text explains that of Peasants, Migrants and Paupers; Rural labour Circulation and Capitalist Production in West India.

The text explains that 1999. ‘Geographical unevenness of India’s Green Revolution’, Journal of Contemporary Asia.

The text explains that postcolonial Developments: Agriculture in the Making of Modern India.

The text explains that farms and Factories; Social Profile of Large Farmers and Rural Industrialists in West India.

The text explains that 2006. ‘Political economy of agrarian distress’.

The text explains that contemporary debate on classes and modes of production in India’.

The text explains that in Dipankar Gupta (Ed.), Social Stratification.

The text explains that hybrid Times, Hybrid People: Culture and Agriculture in South India, Man, Journal of the Royal Anthropological Society.

The text explains that 1999a. ‘Agrarian distress in Bidar: State, Market and Suicides’.

The text explains that harbingers of Rain: Land and Life in south India.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India, Chapter 4: Change and Development in Rural Society

prelims_traps: Do not treat 2011 Census). They make their living from agriculture or related occupations. and 4.1 AgrAriAn Structure: cASte And as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Change and Development in Rural Society; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Change and Development in Rural Society through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 2011 Census). They make their living from agriculture or related occupations. and 4.1 AgrAriAn Structure: cASte And to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
